Learning Persian for speakers of Hindi-Urdu : Lesson 5 - Arabic Grammar for Learning Persian

# Learning Persian for speakers of Hindi-Urdu : Lesson 5 - Arabic Grammar for Learning Persian

### In this article, I continue with explaining the subcontinental Persian language for those who speak Hindi-Urdu. This fifth part teaches the Arabic grammar that is necessary to understand Persian.

![undefined](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!fVaS!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F67200eb0-db6d-4b4d-84b5-78b72ebc90a9_1280x2055.jpeg "undefined")

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The Modern Persian language has lots of Arabic loanwords that form an integral part of everyday spoken and written language and hence a basic knowledge of Arabic grammar will go a long way in improving one’s Persian.

Note : Transliteration Conventions for those who can’t read the Perso-Arabic script :

- The four Arabic consonants **ẓ, dʰ, z, and ḍ** in Arabic are all
  pronounced as “**z**” in Persian and Urdu. E.g. Arabic ***Ramaḍān /
  Maḍbūṭ / Dʰikr / Ẓulm / Ḍarūr / Farḍ*** → Persian ***Ramazān / Mazbūt
  / Zikr / Zulm / Zarūr / Farz.***

- The three Arabic consonants **tʰ**, **s**, and **ṣ** are pronounced
  identiucally in Arabic as “**s**” in Persian. E.g. Arabic ***matʰla /
  sawāl / ṣalat / ṣabr / tʰubūt / salāmat***, → Persian ***masla / sawāl
  / salat / sabr / subūt / salāmat.***

- The Arabic guttural consonants ʔ (hamza) and ʕ (ayn) are silent in
  Persian.

- The Arabic consonant **q** is pronounced identically to **k** in Urdu
  and as **gh** in Modern Iranian Persian.

- The Arabic consonants **ṭ** and **ḥ** are pronounced identically with
  **t** and **h** in Persian and Urdu.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Arabic Triliteral Bases : Roots and Patterns

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Welcome to the fascinating world of Arabic! One of the most exciting things about learning Arabic is discoveringhow its words are built. While Persian belongs to a family of languages called the **Indo-European languages** that include the ancient and modern languages of North India and most of Europe, the Arabic language belongs to an entirely different group of languages called the **Semitic language family**, which includes languages like Hebrew, Akkadian, Amharic (Ethiopic), and Aramaic. What makes Arabic special is its unique system of **roots** and **patterns**, which act like building blocks to create words. By learning this system, you’ll unlock the ability tounderstand and even guess the meaning of many Arabic words which abound in Persian. Let’s dive in and explore this step by step, if you’re learning it for the first time!

In Arabic, most words are built around a core idea, kind of like the heart of the word. This core idea is carried by a group of (usually) **three consonants** called the **root**. The root gives the basic meaning of the word, like “bigness,” “writing,” or “learning.” Then, the **pattern**—made up of vowels, prefixes, suffixes, or extra letters—adds grammatical details, like whether the word is a noun, verb, adjective, or something else. Think of the root as the skeleton of a word, and the pattern as the clothes that dress it up to tell you exactly what it means. By combining different roots and patterns, Arabic creates a huge variety of words, all related to the same core idea. Let’s break it down further with an example.

The root is a set of (usually) three consonants that carry the main meaning of a word. For example, let’s look at the root **K-B-R**, which is all about “greatness” or “bigness.” These three consonants—K, B, and R—are like a code that stays the same in different words, always pointing to the idea of something being big or great. Consider the following words derived out of the root:

- كُبْر ***kubr*** ‘greatness / bigness’

- كَبِير **kabīr** ‘great / big’

- أَكْبَر ***ʔakbar*** ‘greater, greatest / big, biggest’

- تَكْبِير ***takbīr*** ‘professing greatness / bigness’

Notice how all these words share the consonants **K**, **B**, and **R**? That’s the root holding the core meaning together. The differences come from the vowels and extra letters around the root,

The **pattern** is everything else in the word—vowels, prefixes, suffixes, or extra letters—that shapes the root into a specific type of word with a precise meaning. The pattern tells you things like:

- Is this word a noun, verb, or adjective?

- Is it singular or plural?

- Is it active or passive?

- Is it describing a person, an action, or a quality?

Each pattern tweaks the root to give it a specific role in a sentence. By learning common patterns, you’ll start to recognize how words are shaped and what they mean.

The root-and-pattern system is like a puzzle that makes Arabic both logical and creative. Once you know a root’s meaning (like **K-B-R** = “greatness”), you can guess the meanings of new words with that root by looking at their patterns. For example, if you hear a word like مُكَبِّر **mukabbir** (with the **K-B-R** root), you might guess from its root K-B-R and the pattern mu-1-a-2-2-i-3 (where 1, 2, and 3 are the three root consonats K, B, and R) that it has something to do with making something big, like a “magnifier” (and you’d be close ; it can refer to something that amplifies or magnifies!). This system also means that Arabic vocabulary is highly organized. Instead of memorizing thousands of unrelated words, you can learn a few roots and patterns and use them to understand or even create new words.

As another example, take the three consonants Q-T-L that means killing and look at the following Arabic words derived from it and have those three consonants q,t, and l.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PK-S!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a3c998f-ec05-4103-a4cc-5c500f1b944e_1870x1128.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!PK-S!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6a3c998f-ec05-4103-a4cc-5c500f1b944e_1870x1128.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Persian borrows only nouns and adjectives from Arabic while maintaining its verbal structure intact. Most Persian compound verbs, even if they are borrowed from Arabic, only have their non-verbal element from Arabic while the verbal part is still Persian. Thus, it is enough to learn only the grammar of Arabic nouns and adjectives for learning Persian. Note that all Persian Muslim authors knew their Arabic very well and many times never hesitated to insert full Arabic phrases and sentences in between like any erudite English author never hestiates to quote Latin maxims and expressions. But in this article, I will be focusing only on those Arabic loanwords in Persian which are only nouns and adjectives.

Arabic grammarians denote the three consonants of a root by the letters **F** ف, **ʕ** ع, and **L** ل respectively. Thus, the words ***kabīr*** and ***wazīr*** follow the same ***faʕīl*** pattern.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Present Active Participle & fāʿil فَاعِل Pattern : Doer of the Verb

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The first pattern that we will see is called the فَاعِل **fāʿil** pattern **C1-ā-C2-i-C3** where C1, C2, C3 are the three consonants of the root. Here’s what that means:

- **C1, C2, C3** are the three consonants of the root, which carry the
  core meaning (like “writing” or “learning”).

- The vowels **ā** (a long “a” sound) and **i** (a short “i” sound) are
  added between the consonants.

- The result is a word that describes **a person or thing that does the
  action** related to the root’s meaning.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wfJg!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F93eee6f2-e641-4562-9c39-060a854de388_1810x1018.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!wfJg!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F93eee6f2-e641-4562-9c39-060a854de388_1810x1018.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In the right side of the picture above, the red, green, and blue C denote the three consonants of the verb root respectively. For example, if the root is about “writing,” the *fāʿil* pattern turns it into “writer” (someone who writes). If the root is about “cooking,” it becomes “cook” (someone who cooks). It’s like adding “-**er**” or “-**or**” in English to make words like “baker” or “doer” or “actor”. If the root means “to be / to do X”, then this pattern means “someone (or something) who is / who does X”. Plus, it’s similar to English! Just like we say “teach” → “teacher” or “act” → “actor,” Arabic uses *fāʿil* to say “**K-T-B** write” → “***kātib (***writer)” or “**D-R-S** study” → “***dāris (***student)”. This makes it easier to relate to if you’re familiar with English word endings. In each case, the *fāʿil* pattern takes the root’s core idea (writing, studying, cooking) and turns it into a word for **the person who does that action**. It’s a simple but powerful way to create nouns that describe people (or sometimes things) based on what they do. This is equivalent to the present participle suffix in Persian which is **indá نده**. Famous known examples of Arabic words of this pattern are given below in the green boxes.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VUtV!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6259a1bb-9e74-43ec-93ae-5a9c54e63900_2138x1206.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!VUtV!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6259a1bb-9e74-43ec-93ae-5a9c54e63900_2138x1206.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

# The Past Passive Participle & mafʿūl مَفْعُول Pattern : Object of the Verb

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While the previous pattern of a root verb X meant someone or something who does X or who is X, this pattern مَفْعُول **ma**-C1-C2-**ū**-C3 means someone (or something) who is X-ed. It refers to the object that suffers the action of the verb. It is equivalent to the past passive participle suffix **á** **ه** in Persian. Here’s what the notation means:

- **C1, C2, C3** are the three consonants of the root, which carry the
  core meaning (like “protecting” or“knowing”).

- The prefix **ma-** and the vowels **ū** are added around the
  consonants.

- The result is a word that means **someone or something that has been
  affected by the action** of the root.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JwgW!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbd05e232-f28c-43e9-bf89-f91aa841ab8d_1810x1020.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!JwgW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbd05e232-f28c-43e9-bf89-f91aa841ab8d_1810x1020.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In each case, the *mafʿūl* pattern takes the root’s core idea (protecting, knowing, writing) and turns it into a word for **the person or thing that receives the action**. Notice how it contrasts with the *fāʿil* pattern, which describes the *doer* of the action.

It is equivalent to the “-ed / -(e)n / -t” suffix in English that is reflected in verbal adjectives like grown, oppressed, depressed, painted, envied, forgiven, etc. For example, if the Arabic word حَافِظ ***ḥāfiẓ*** means “*protector*“ (someone who protects), then the word مَحْفُوظ ***maḥfūẓ*** means “*protected*“ (someone / something that is protected). Famous known examples of Arabic words of this pattern are given below in the red boxes.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SFHZ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe633c916-fa97-4bbd-b945-81453c499f02_2138x1206.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SFHZ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe633c916-fa97-4bbd-b945-81453c499f02_2138x1206.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

# The Nouns of Place

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Now, we will see nouns derived from a verb root that means a place (or sometimes time) for doing the verb. These come in two types of pattern.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!N_IE!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F49d44387-0c66-4394-893c-1aec02dab7c3_1816x1024.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!N_IE!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F49d44387-0c66-4394-893c-1aec02dab7c3_1816x1024.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The mafʿal\[at\] مَفْعَل / مَفْعَلة pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some verb roots take the pattern for the noun of place as **ma**-C1-C2-**a**-C3-\[**at**\]. The feminine suffix “at” is added for some roots and is not added for others and is optional for some. Some famous examples of Arabic words taking this pattern are given below in the gray boxes.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BxX_!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb85542f6-8bbe-4546-b286-e17de35bffe3_2134x1212.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!BxX_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb85542f6-8bbe-4546-b286-e17de35bffe3_2134x1212.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AwJ8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F54ce4173-e2d4-4d2b-8cbc-2c7facbadfb3_2132x1204.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!AwJ8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F54ce4173-e2d4-4d2b-8cbc-2c7facbadfb3_2132x1204.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The mafʿil مَفْعِل pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some verb roots take the pattern for the noun of place as **ma**-C1-C2-**i**-C3. Some famous examples of Arabic words taking this pattern are given below in the gray boxes.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Afsl!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0e4c53bd-29cb-413a-9ca8-4be7f29a1bf3_2134x1204.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Afsl!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0e4c53bd-29cb-413a-9ca8-4be7f29a1bf3_2134x1204.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Quiz :

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!e4oC!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F32b2145a-9f35-4fce-878e-a69a7b2cffb6_1816x1024.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!e4oC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F32b2145a-9f35-4fce-878e-a69a7b2cffb6_1816x1024.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Answers : one who is prepared, the kingdom, the place of killing (execution chamber in Modern Standard Arabic)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# The Abstract Noun

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Now, we will see the pattern for abstract nouns for verb roots. If the verb root means X, the abstract noun is a noun that means “the act of doing X / being X”. It functions as the infinitive form of the verb in Persian that ends in **dan/tan**. However, in Arabic, this is a very irregular formation because it comes in so many patterns. In this introductory tutorial however, we will see the four major patterns and important particular examples of other patterns. The major patterns are first summarised below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xZn2!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe1e5296d-dea2-484d-9be9-80893de2ccbb_1808x1038.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xZn2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fe1e5296d-dea2-484d-9be9-80893de2ccbb_1808x1038.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The faʿl فَعْل pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Verbal nouns for many roots take this pattern which is given by C1-**a**-C2-C3. Ocassionally, the feminine suffix \[at\] too can come at the end of this pattern. Famous examples of this pattern are given in the yellow boxes below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!cezx!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F3d72a6b2-3f34-4267-966e-8400e778c442_2144x1206.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lYsF!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F390421b2-d9e2-48ce-9879-3863732129d4_2138x1212.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lYsF!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F390421b2-d9e2-48ce-9879-3863732129d4_2138x1212.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SgMX!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4fa6835d-b54b-464d-b335-67131c53ebcc_2140x1206.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!SgMX!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4fa6835d-b54b-464d-b335-67131c53ebcc_2140x1206.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!V9wC!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F64501c7e-5a8e-401a-8a3d-7815c8eb6894_2144x1210.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The fiʿl فِعْل pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Verbal nouns for many roots take this pattern which is given by C1-**i**-C2-C3. Ocassionally, the feminine suffix \[at\] too can come at the end of this pattern. Famous examples of this pattern are given in the yellow boxes below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!forp!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Feff0bd78-bf76-4160-bfb9-429f6e16a9e7_2136x1208.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The fuʿl فُعْل pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Verbal nouns for many roots take this pattern which is given by C1-**u**-C2-C3. Ocassionally, the feminine suffix \[at\] too can come at the end of this pattern. Famous examples of this pattern are given in the yellow boxes below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!LQ36!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4a6cde59-f2b8-421b-a67b-9f5159ec002c_2136x1208.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tj5J!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8aacef44-fe59-4396-b5cd-45951d051c0b_2146x1200.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tj5J!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8aacef44-fe59-4396-b5cd-45951d051c0b_2146x1200.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### The fuʿ**ū**l فُعُول pattern

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Verbal nouns for some roots take this pattern which is given by C1-**u**-C2-**ū**-C3. Famous examples of this pattern are given in the yellow boxes below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0vNj!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F191fefe0-5ede-4aae-adb7-7d4f18035db0_2152x1222.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

### Irregular verbal nouns

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some roots form verbal nouns with patterns that are irregular and are taken only by it or a small group of it. There are dozens of such patterns but an important bunch of then that most frequently occur are given in the yellow box below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nKcS!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fb91a91cb-990d-4740-b325-00cdfd8843a1_2138x698.heic)

# The Intensive Adjective

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Next, we will see the patterns for what is called an intensive adjective. The intensive noun or an adjective associated with a verb root in Arabic technically denotes to someone that performs the action of the verb intensely or frequently or repeatedly or habitually or professionally. But sometimes, the intensification is lost and is only conventional rather than actual. This form too can come in many patterns but here I will give ony the four important and most frequent patterns for the intensive adjectives/nouns.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7Xv3!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F61f5d55f-78b9-497d-a302-c417be3edb76_1808x1022.heic)

# The Nouns of Tool

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Now, we will see nouns derived from a verb root that means a tool or an instrument for doing the verb. These come in two types of patterns.

# Derived Forms of Verbs

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

While Persian has comfortably adopted thousands of Arabic nouns and adjectives, many of the most elegant, precise, and commonly used ones actually come from **higher verbal forms** (Forms II through XVI) in Arabic. At first glance, the Arabic “form system” may seem complex — a series of patterned modifications (Form 1, Form 2, Form 3, … up to Form 10) that Arabic applies to its roots. However, for Persian speakers, mastering this system is incredibly rewarding and surprisingly practical. Instead of treating every borrowed word as an isolated item to memorize, understanding the forms allows you to recognize patterns, predict meanings, and correctly use the **active and passive participles** that have entered Persian in large numbers. Words like **مدرّس** (mudarris), **مستقبل** (mustaqbal), **متخصص** (mutakhassiṣ), **منتشر** (muntashir), or **مستقل** (mustaqill) are not random; they follow predictable morphological patterns tied to their form. Once you grasp these patterns, a whole new layer of Arabic-derived vocabulary in Persian literature, journalism, academia, and religious texts suddenly becomes logical, memorable, and much easier to master.

As we saw, Arabic words are built on a system of **roots** (usually three consonants, called radicals) and **patterns** (awzān). A root carries a core meaning, and different patterns modify that meaning to create nouns, adjectives, or participles. The “forms” (awzān or abniya) are a specific set of ten patterns applied to verbs and their derivatives. What we saw so far are derived words in Form I which is the simplest and most basic.

Now, we are going to repeat the derivations of the most imporant among these higher forms and see their verbal nouns, active participles, and passive participles. These higher forms numbered from II onwards add prefixes, infixes, or gemination (doubling of consonants) to create nuanced meanings such as causation, intensification, reciprocity, reflexivity, or passivity. For Persian students, the key insight is this: you do not need to conjugate verbs. You only need to recognize the **participle patterns** (verbal nouns, active and passive participles) that have been borrowed directly into Persian as nouns or adjectives. These participles function exactly like Persian words — they take Persian plurals (ها or Arabic broken plurals), enter iḍāfa constructions, and agree in gender where relevant. Understanding the form tells you instantly whether a word like **مُدَرِّس** (mudarris) means “the one who teaches” (active, intensive). This predictability turns thousands of seemingly arbitrary Arabic loans into a systematic family.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Form II : Intensification and Causation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Among all the Arabic verbal forms borrowed into Persian, **Form II** stands out as one of the most practical and frequentlyencountered. If you have ever used words like **مدرس** (**mudarris** – teacher), **معلم** (**muʿallim** – instructor), **منظم** (**munaẓẓam** –organized), or **مکرر** (**mukarrar** – repeated) in your Persian reading or writing, you have already been working with Form II derivatives ; often without realizing it.For Persian speakers, Form II is especially motivating because it generates a large number of **professional titles**, **agents ofchange**, and **precise descriptive adjectives** that enrich formal Persian, academic texts, journalism, and religious discourse.Instead of memorizing hundreds of isolated Arabic loans, understanding Form II allows you to see clear patterns: manywords that describe “someone who causes something to happen” or “something that has been made to happen intensively”come from this form.This predictability transforms vocabulary learning from rote memorization into logical pattern recognition. Once you master Form II’s structures, you will instantly recognize and correctly use dozens of common Persian words, predict their meaningsfrom known roots, handle gender agreement, and choose the right plurals in formal writing. Form II is, in many ways, thebridge that connects basic Arabic roots to the sophisticated, causative vocabulary that Persian has adopted soenthusiastically.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!U5Xq!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6f9549a0-c9db-479e-bac8-90ce7c55c38c_1748x544.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!U5Xq!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6f9549a0-c9db-479e-bac8-90ce7c55c38c_1748x544.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form II is created by **doubling the second root letter** (adding a shadda). This morphological change typically adds two main shades of meaning:

1.  **Causative** — to cause someone or something to perform the basic
    action (turning “to study” into “to teach”).

2.  **Intensive** — to do the action more forcefully, repeatedly, or
    thoroughly (turning “to kill” into “to massacre”).

For Persian students, we focus on three key derivatives that function as nouns and adjectives in Persian:

- **Verbal Noun** : تَفْعِيل (tafʕīl) — the act of causing or intensifying
  the action.

- **Active Participle :** مُفَعِّل (mufaʕʕil) — the person or agent who
  causes the action.

- **Passive Participle** : مُفَعَّل (mufaʕʕal) — the thing or person that
  has been caused to undergo the action.

To illustrate the contrast between Form I and Form II verbs, a comparison between their verbal nouns is given below for three examples.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9qGH!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9109ca89-6a78-42a9-ae21-c8bf6d5bac30_1646x978.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!9qGH!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9109ca89-6a78-42a9-ae21-c8bf6d5bac30_1646x978.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

To summarise, we have

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Zx3c!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F339c7aa8-45f2-4232-ae84-c16b50df51e6_1878x754.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Zx3c!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F339c7aa8-45f2-4232-ae84-c16b50df51e6_1878x754.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Note : The difference between the active and passive participle in Form II is just the vowel before the third consonant of the root.

> Hereafter, for all forms of II and higher, the active and passive
> participle differ only in the vowel before the third consonant of the
> root. Active participles have an “i” and passive participles have an
> “a” before the third consonant of the root and otherwise, they are
> identical.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Form III : Intensification and Causation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If you have come across Persian words such as **مبارزه** (**mubāriza** – struggle or fight), **معامله** (muʿāmala – transaction or dealing), **مشاهده** (**mushāhada** – observation or watching), or **مخاطب** (**mukhāṭab** – addressee), you are already using derivatives from **Arabic Form III**. Form III is particularly valuable for Persian speakers because it supplies a rich set of nouns and adjectives that express **interaction**, **reciprocity**, **directed effort toward someone or something**, and **mutual actions**. In fields like law, diplomacy, media, literature, religion, and everyday formal Persian, these words add precision and elegance. They allow you to describe relationships between people or entities - fighting against, dealing with, observing, addressing, or competing with ; in ways that basic Persian vocabulary or simpler Arabic borrowings cannot always capture as neatly. Understanding Form III motivates Persian learners by revealing yet another systematic layer in the Arabic-derived lexicon. Instead of treating these terms as random loans, you learn to recognize the long ā (alif) pattern that signals “action directed toward another” or “mutual engagement.” This pattern recognition helps you guess meanings from familiar roots, use the words correctly in iḍāfa constructions, handle gender agreement, and choose appropriate plurals in academic or official writing. Form III complements Form II beautifully: while Form II often creates causative professionals (“the one who causes X”), Form III creates interactive roles and processes (“the one who does X with/toward another”).

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!reBw!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F789cac65-1594-45b7-9849-748923a04421_1716x716.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!reBw!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F789cac65-1594-45b7-9849-748923a04421_1716x716.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form III is formed by inserting an **alif (ا)** after the first root letter, creating a long ā sound. The typical semantic contributions include:

- Trying or directing effort toward an object (often with a sense of
  reciprocity when the action is mutual).

- Competing, contending, or interacting with another.

- Doing the action intensively in relation to someone else.

For Persian students, the most useful derivatives that function as nouns and adjectives are:

- **Verbal Noun** : Two common patterns — **مُفَاعَلَة** (**mufāʿala**) or
  **فِعَال** (**fiʿāl**). These denote the act of mutual or directed
  interaction.

- **Active Participle** **مُفَاعِل** (**mufāʿil**) — the person or agent
  who directs the action toward another or participates mutually.

- **Passive Participle** : **مُفَاعَل** (**mufāʿal**) — the one or thing
  that receives the directed or reciprocal action.

The meanings are illustrated below with some examples.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!Lwkc!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Faa407743-6869-48b0-8bc1-e927a2f58783_1738x986.heic)

## Form IV : Causation & Preparation

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If you frequently encounter Persian words such as **مسلمان** (**musalmān** – Muslim), **مؤمن** (**muʾmin** – believer), **اخبار** (**akhbār** – news), **احکام** (aḥkām – rulings), **اعلام** (**iʿlām** – announcement), or **مخرج** (**mukhrij** – director/extractor), you are already engaging with derivatives from **Arabic Form IV**. Form IV holds special importance for Persian speakers because it supplies many **core religious**, **philosophical**, **scientific**, and **administrative** terms that form the backbone of formal Persian, especially in Islamic scholarship, law, media, and academia. These words often carry a strong **causative** sense (“to cause someone/something to do the basic action”) or add **emphasis** and **transitivity** to the root meaning. For Persian learners, Form IV is highly motivating: its derivatives are among the most elegant and frequently used Arabic loans in elevated Persian registers. Recognizing the characteristic **إ** (hamza + alif) prefix and the **مُفْعِل** / **مُفْعَل** (**mufʕil / mufʕal**) participle patterns allows you to connect familiar roots to precise, causative vocabulary. This turns scattered words into a coherent system, helping you predict meanings and use them in formal writing. Form IV beautifully complements Form II (intensive causation with doubling) by providing another layer of “making something happen,” often with a more declarative or emphatic tone that enriches religious and intellectual discourse in Persian.

Form IV is created by prefixing **أَ** (hamza with fatḥa) to the root and placing a sukūn (no vowel) on the first radical. Typical semantic contributions include:

- Causative: turning an intransitive action into a transitive one (e.g.,
  “to know” → “to inform”).

- Emphasis or declaration of a state.

- Bringing something into existence or highlighting a quality.

For Persian students, focus on the three key derivatives that function actively as nouns and adjectives:

- **Verbal Noun** : **إِفْعَال** (**ifʕāl**) — the act of causing or
  emphasizing the action.

- **Active Participle** : **مُفْعِل** (**mufʕil**) — the person or agent
  who causes or emphasizes the action.

- **Passive Participle** : **مُفْعَل** (**mufʕal**) — the thing or person
  that has been caused to undergo the action or brought into a certain
  state.

These patterns integrate seamlessly into Persian and appear constantly in religious texts, news, legal documents, and scholarly writing.

Some important verbal nouns in Form IV are given below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RB2H!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2d13a210-c24e-404b-913e-f6c6a99192f7_1808x1028.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RB2H!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F2d13a210-c24e-404b-913e-f6c6a99192f7_1808x1028.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

For present active participles, we have

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8XT2!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F94db1670-9218-464f-b679-5e82081cb04b_1808x1014.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nXiB!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fbaa3e8c9-3758-4791-8acc-661013df51ca_1896x948.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Form V : Reflexive Intensive

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

If you regularly use or read Persian words such as **متخصص** (**mutakhassiṣ** – specialist/expert), **متقدم** (**mutaqaddim** – advanced, progressive), **متحرک** (**mutaḥarrik** – moving, dynamic, mobile), **متکبر** (**mutakabbir** – arrogant), you are already working with derivatives from **Arabic Form V**. Form V is one of the most valuable forms for Persian speakers because it generates a large number of **modern, dynamic adjectives** and **professional or personal descriptors** that express **self-directed**, **reflexive**, or **progressive/intensive** actions. These words often describe states that someone or something has brought upon itself or that have developed gradually. For Persian learners, Form V is particularly motivating: its clear **مُتَفَعِّل** pattern (with the prefix مُتَ and the doubled middle radical) makes it easy to spot and remember. Once you recognize this pattern, you can unlock dozens of common words without memorizing them individually. You will start predicting meanings from known roots (e.g., “to specialize” → **متخصص**), handle gender agreement correctly, use the right plurals in academic contexts, and appreciate how these terms add depth to descriptions of progress, expertise, and personal qualities. Form V nicely complements Form II (external causation: “the one who causes teaching”) by shifting the focus inward or progressively (“the one who has specialized himself” or “the advanced one”).

Form V is morphologically the reflexive or intensive version of Form II. Typical semantic contributions include:

- Reflexive: the subject applies the action to itself.

- Gradual development or acquiring a quality.

- Intensive or pretentious application of the action.

For Persian students, the key derivatives that function as nouns and adjectives in Persian are:

- **Verbal Noun** : **تَفَعُّل** (**tafaʕʕul**) — the process of
  self-application or gradual development.

- **Active Participle** : **مُتَفَعِّل** (**mutafaʕʕil**) — the person or
  thing that has applied the action to itself or acquired the quality
  (often used as a noun or adjective).

- **Passive Participle** : **مُتَفَعَّل** (**mutafaʕʕal**) — rarer in
  borrowing, but occasionally appears for the result of the
  self-directed action.

These patterns are highly productive in Persian and blend effortlessly with Persian grammar. Some important verbal nouns in Form V are given below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TQg_!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F43d7aa59-ce15-457f-8ac6-6f4639f5a689_1818x524.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TQg_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F43d7aa59-ce15-457f-8ac6-6f4639f5a689_1818x524.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!li6F!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fba1fd56b-0571-4343-b614-aa3cfb262bfb_1808x1020.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!0fP_!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F0ec2861f-d3c5-44e8-a249-86130b2d66b9_1844x958.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Form VII : Passive Reflexive

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form VII is less frequent than Forms II, V, or VIII in Persian and Urdu borrowings, but it still contributes useful **intransitive**, **passive**, and **reflexive** nouns and adjectives. If you encounter words such as **منقلب** (**munqalib** – overturned, revolutionary), **منفصل** (**munfaṣil** – separate, detached), **منقطع** (**munqaṭiʿ** – cut off, interrupted), or **منفعل** (**munfaʿil** – affected, annoyed), you are dealing with derivatives from **Arabic Form VII**. For Persian and Urdu speakers, Form VII is motivating because it provides precise terms for **states of being affected**, **separation**, **change of state**, or **intransitive processes** without an obvious external agent. These words often appear in historical, political, emotional, or technical contexts ; for example, describing revolutions, emotional reactions, or interrupted processes. While not as common as the more productive higher forms, mastering Form VII helps you recognize a consistent pattern that adds nuance to formal reading in literature, news, religious texts, and academic writing. Understanding Form VII reveals another layer of the Arabic root-and-pattern system. Once identified, you can often infer meanings like “to become X-ed” or “to be affected by X” from familiar roots.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TZs8!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4d3331fd-f78b-4970-bc1b-c904fdf29d55_1722x534.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!TZs8!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4d3331fd-f78b-4970-bc1b-c904fdf29d55_1722x534.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form VII is typically built on Form I by prefixing **اِنْ** (in-). The basic verb pattern is **اِنْفَعَلَ** (infaʿala). It is almost always **intransitive** and often carries **passive** or **reflexive** meaning — the subject undergoes or performs the action upon itself, without a direct object.Typical semantic contributions include:

- Passive sense: “to be X-ed” or “to become X-ed”.

- Reflexive or middle voice: the subject is affected internally.

- Intransitive change of state (e.g., to break off, to turn over, to
  separate).

For Persian and Urdu students, the most useful derivatives are:

- **Verbal Noun** : **اِنْفِعَال** (**infiʕāl**) — the process or state of
  being affected or changed.

- **Active Participle**: **مُنْفَعِل** (**munfaʕil**) — the one or thing
  that has undergone the passive/reflexive action (often used as an
  adjective describing a state). Since the normal verb itself is
  passive, there is no separate passive participle in this form.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!D4Ac!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d4caf0e-532c-4c4f-a511-2da03b22aefa_1734x980.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!D4Ac!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F1d4caf0e-532c-4c4f-a511-2da03b22aefa_1734x980.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Note: Form VII emphasizes the result or the internal experience of a verb as the passive voice does in English. Some famous verbal nouns from this form are given below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tJY0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F22ef35a6-da1d-41b3-ae48-6c4ed095f8e6_1810x670.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!tJY0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F22ef35a6-da1d-41b3-ae48-6c4ed095f8e6_1810x670.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Among the participles, the only famous example is **munqalib** which means “one that is capsized”. So, not much elaboration is given for this part. To summarise, we have

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gTVK!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd7120a9c-3d3f-412b-9ffa-ef7ae99d4d8b_1882x694.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!gTVK!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd7120a9c-3d3f-412b-9ffa-ef7ae99d4d8b_1882x694.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Form VIII : Collective Deliberative

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form VIII stands as one of the most elegant and productive sources of Arabic-derived vocabulary in both Persian and Urdu. If you frequently encounter words such as **مجتمع** (**mujtamaʿ** – complex, society, housing estate), **منتشر** (**muntashir** – published, disseminated), **مختصر** (**mukhtaṣar** – brief, summarized), **معتقد** (**muʿtaqad** – believed, conviction), or **منتظر** (**muntaẓar** – awaited, expected), you are already using derivatives from **Arabic Form VIII**. For Persian and Urdu speakers, Form VIII is especially motivating because it generates sophisticated **social**, **collective**, **transformative**, and **acquisitive** terms that enrich discourse. These words often carry nuanced meanings of **reflexive action** (doing something to or for oneself), **effortful acquisition**, **gathering**, **publication**, or **transformation**. Form VIII complements earlier forms beautifully: while Form II creates external causation and Form V creates self-directed progress, Form VIII often highlights reflexive effort or collective transformation.

Form VIII is created by inserting a **ت** (t) after the first root letter of the basic Form I verb, often with a hamza (إ) at the beginning. This morphological change typically adds meanings such as:

- **Effortful acquisition** — obtaining or achieving something with
  deliberate effort.

- **Gathering or collective action** — bringing things or people
  together.

- **Publication or dissemination** — spreading or making something
  public.

- **Transformative or middle voice** — the subject undergoes or
  participates in a change.

Persian has borrowed heavily from these nuances because they elegantly express complex social, intellectual, and collective processes that complement native Persian vocabulary.For Persian students, you do not need to learn verb conjugations. Focus instead on the three key derivatives that function as nouns and adjectives in Persian:

- **Verbal Noun** : **اِفْتِعَال** (**iftiʿāl**) — the process or result of
  the reflexive/transformative action.

- **Active Participle**: **مُفْتَعِل** (**muftaʿil**) — the agent who
  gathers, acquires, or strives collectively.

- **Passive Participle**: **مُفْتَعَل** (**muftaʿal**) — the thing or person
  that has been transformed, gathered, published, or affected.

Popular verbal nouns in this category are shown below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!7kd0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F692eb67f-f571-4eea-94b0-bdf5d6fef3f4_1804x792.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some important active participles in this category are :

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!beh6!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F9457868a-370f-4b1d-adae-081e0deda410_1802x644.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

## Form X : Aspiration

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Form X represents one of the most modern, abstract, and intellectually rich layers of Arabic vocabulary borrowed into Persian. If you have ever used or read words such as **استقلال** (**istiqlāl** – independence), **استخراج** (**istikhrāj** – extraction), **مستخدم** (**mustakhdam** – employee), or **استعمال** (**istiʿmāl** – usage), you are already engaging with **Arabic Form X** derivatives. For Persian speakers, Form X is exceptionally motivating because it supplies precise terms for **aspiration**, **potential**, **consideration**, **seeking**, and **technical or philosophical concepts**. These words dominate discussions in politics (independence), science and technology (extraction, usage), philosophy (future-oriented thinking), law, and academia. Persian adopted them because they allow speakers to express nuanced ideas of “seeking X,” “considering something as X,” or “requesting/achieving a quality” with elegance and clarity that native Persian morphology often expresses less concisely. For example, the root **Kh-D-M** means service or work and hence its form X verbal noun **istikhdām** means seeking or aspiring to work which connects to its actual meaning of employment. The power of Form X lies in its highly recognizable pattern: the prefix **اِسْتَ** (ista-).

Form X is built by prefixing **اِسْتَ** (ista-) to the root. This form typically conveys:

- **Seeking or requesting** something for oneself (e.g., seeking
  independence).

- **Considering or deeming** something to have a certain quality.

- **Aspiration** or striving toward a state.

- **Extraction** or bringing something out (in technical senses).

In Persian, you focus only on the derivatives that function as nouns and adjectives:

- **Verbal Noun** : **اِسْتِفْعَال** (**istifʕāl**) — the act of seeking,
  considering, or extracting.

- **Active Participle**: **مُسْتَفْعِل** (**mustafʕil**) — the one who seeks
  or considers.

- **Passive Participle**: **مُسْتَفْعَل** (**mustafʕal**) — the thing sought,
  considered, or extracted.

The consistent **مُسْتَ** **musta** / **isti** **ست** prefix makes these words easy to spot in texts. Only the verbal noun is often used in Persian and hence many examples of it are given below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RcW0!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80a2c567-302f-4c56-aba1-c387a0ca8f6c_1822x1024.heic)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!RcW0!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F80a2c567-302f-4c56-aba1-c387a0ca8f6c_1822x1024.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

But, in case you want to know the participles, read the forms in the summary below. To summarise, we have the forms of Q-T-L (meaning : to kill) in form X that means risking getting killed.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!nOrI!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6e629418-cb81-47cd-b0cd-28365bc3ce45_1889x723.jpeg)

# Revision of Patterns

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

In this section, we will revise all the patterns of nouns and adjectives that we have seen so far.

Now, all the participles and verbal nouns are summarised together below.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xYnY!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Fd18c8685-cc89-4e22-a4c7-568c9a4aba30_1882x872.heic)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Note the inner logic behind these participles and how participles of the same color are related with just extra insertions in the middle.

These are the most frequent patterns of Arabic nouns and adjectives and whenever you see any Arabic word in Persian or Urdu, think for a moment and analyse which pattern it follows and identify the root.

------------------------------------------------------------------------

# Arabic Broken Plurals

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Persian has borrowed thousands of Arabic nouns and adjectives, especially in formal, academic, religious, literary, and media contexts. Arabic nouns form their plurals in the same way as English nouns broadly. In English, there are two broad ways to form the plural.

- **Sound Plural** : Add the ending **-(e)s** to the singular. (*book* →
  *books*, *ship* → *ships*, *document* → *documents*, *church* →
  *churches*)

- **Broken Plural** : Change the vowels inside the singular word to form
  the plural (*man* → *men*, *goose* → *geese*, *tooth* → *teeth*,
  *mouse* → *mice*)

In English, one cannot escape the broken plurals as they are very frequently used. Similarly, in Arabic, there are two ways of forming the plurals for nouns.

- **Sound Plural** : Add the ending **-ūn** (masc) **/ āt** (fem) to the
  singular (*muslim* → *muslimūn*, *muslimat* → *muslimāt*).   
  **NOTE** : Derivatives of nouns and participles from Form II and
  higher forms, use only sound plurals.

- **Broken Plural** : Change the vowels inside the singular word to form
  the plural (*kāfir* → *kuffār*, *lafẓ* → *ʔalfāz*, *waqt* → *ʔawqāt*,
  *kitāb* → *kutub*, *masjid* → *masājid*)

Unfortunately, most nouns in Arabic take the broken plural forms and like in English, the broken plural of a noun cannot be predicted using any simple rule by its singular form and hence has to be memorised separately. Now, what does Persian do to form the plurals of Arabic nouns that it borrows? While you can often add the simple Persian plural ending **-hā** (ها) in everyday speech (for example, **kitābhā** for “books”), formal Persian also sometimes strongly prefers the original Arabic **broken plurals**. As already said, these plurals do not add an ending; instead, they change the internal vowels and structure of the word. This is why you encounter **kutub** instead of *kitābhā*, **ʿulūm** instead of *ʿilmhā*, or **aḥkām** instead of *ḥukmhā* in university textbooks, newspaper headlines, book titles, legal documents, and classical literature. Learning broken plurals is one of the most practical and rewarding skills for Persian students. It allows you to:

- Recognize plurals instantly without memorizing every word
  individually.

- Use elegant and authentic forms in iḍāfat constructions (e.g.,
  **ʿulūm-e insānī** – human sciences, **kutub-e darsī** – textbooks).

- Avoid common mistakes such as mixing Persian **-hā** with an already
  broken plural in formal writing.

- Read and write formal Persian with greater speed, accuracy, and
  stylistic confidence.

Arabic broken plurals follow dozens of patterns but the most frequently occuring plural forms are limited to a small number of predictable patterns called **awzān**. Once you learn these patterns and the most frequent examples, you will master the majority of Arabic plurals used in Persian. Below are the **most common broken plural patterns** in Persian, each with clear explanations and many high-frequency examples.

### **فُعُول (fuʿūl)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This pattern is one among the most frequently occuring in Arabic .

- **علم** (**ʿilm** – knowledge) → **علوم** (**ʿulūm** – sciences)  
  Persian usage: علوم انسانی (*ʿulūm-e insānī* – human sciences)

- **حرف** (**ḥarf** – letter) → **حروف** (**ḥurūf** – letters)  
  Persian usage: حروف الفبا (*ḥurūf-e alifbā* – alphabet letters)

- **درس** (**dars** – lesson) → **دروس** (**durūs** – lessons)  
  Persian usage: دروس دانشگاهی (*durūs-e dānishgāhī* – university
  courses)

- **شرط** (**sharṭ** – condition) → **شروط** (**shurūṭ** – conditions)

- **قصر** (**qaṣr** – palace) → **قصور** (**quṣūr** – palaces)

- **بحر** (**baḥr** – sea) → **بحور** (**buḥūr** – seas)

- **قلب** (**qulb** – heart) → **قلوب** (**qulūb** – hearts)

- **سيف** (**sayf** – sword) → **سيوف** (**suyūf** – swords)

- **مالك** (**mālik** – king) → **ملوك** (**mulūk** – kings)

- **شهر** (**shahr** – month) → **شهور** (**shuhūr** – months), → also
  **أشهر** (**ashhur** - months)

### **أَفْعَال (afʿāl)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This is by far the most frequent pattern in formal Persian**.**

- **حكم** (**ḥukm** – ruling, command) → **أحكام** (**aḥkām** –
  rulings)  
  Persian usage: أحكام شرعی (*aḥkām*-*e* *sharʿī* – sharia rulings)

- **خبر** (**khabar** – news, report) → **أخبار** (**akhbār** – news)  
  Persian usage: اخبار جهان (*akhbār*-*e* *jahān* – world news)

- **قلم** (**qalam** – pen) → **أقلام** (**aqlām** – pens)

- **ولد** (**walad** – male child) → **أولاد** (**awlād** - children)

- **فعل** (**fiʿl** – action) → **أفعال** (**afʿāl** – actions)

- **سبب** (**sabab** – cause, reason) → **أسباب** (**asbāb** – causes,
  reasons)

- **قول** (**qawl** – saying) → **أقوال** (**aqwāl** – sayings)

- **وقت** (**waqt** – time) → **أوقات** (**awqāt** – times)

- **مطر** (**maṭar** – rain) → **أمطار** (**amṭār** – rains)

### **فِعَال (fiʿāl)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Quite a bit of words take this pattern too.

- **كلب** (**kalb** – dog) → **كلاب** (**kilāb** – dogs)

- **رجل** (**rajul** – man) → **رجال** (**rijāl** – men)

- **جبل** (**jabal** – mountain) → **جبال** (**jibāl** – mountains)

### **فُعُل (fuʿul)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Quite a bit of words take this pattern too.

- **كتاب** (**kitāb** – book) → **كتب** (**kutub** – books)

- **رسول** (**rasūl** – messenger) → **رسل** (**rusul** – messengers)

- **مدينة** (**madīnat** – city) → **مدن** (**mudun** – cities)  
  Persian usage: مدن بزرگ (*mudun-e buzurg* – large cities)

- **سفينة** (**safīnat** – large ship) → **سفن** (**sufun** – ships)

### **Feminine plurals ending in -āt ات**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Most feminine nouns (ending in “**at**”) form their plural regularly in the sound way by changing the ending **at** → **āt**.

- **حكومة** (**ḥukūmat** – government) → **حكومات** (**ḥukūmāt** –
  governments)

- **لغة** (**lughat** – language) → **لغات** (**lughāt** – languages)  
  Persian usage: لغات عربی (*lughāt-e ʿarabī* – Arabic words)

- **جماعة** (**jamāʿa** – group, community) → **جماعات** (**jamāʿāt** –
  groups)

### **فُعَل (fuʿal)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Some nouns take the broken plural pattern **فُعَل fuʿal.**

- **دولة** (**dawlat** – state, government) → **دول** (**duwal** –
  states)  
  Persian usage: دول اسلامی (*duwal-e islāmī* – Islamic states)

### **مَفَاعِل (mafāʿil)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This pattern is used for nouns of place.

- **مكتب** (**maktab** – office, school) → **مكاتب** (**makātib** –
  offices)

- **مصنع** (**maṣnaʿ** – factory) → **مصانع** (**maṣāniʿ** – factories)

- **مسجد** (**masjid** – mosque) → **مساجد** (**masājid** – mosques)

### **مَفَاعِيل (mafāʿīl)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This pattern is used for nouns of instrument.

- **مفتاح** (**miftāḥ** – key) → **مفاتيح** (**mafātīḥ** – keys)

- **مصباح** (**miṣbāḥ** – lamp) → **مصابيح** (**maṣābīḥ** – lamps)

### **فُعَّال (fuʿʿāl)**

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

This pattern is used by the majority of form I present active participles.

- **كاتب** (**kātib** – writer) → **كتاب** (**kuttāb** – writers)

- **كَافِر** (**kāfir**) → **كُفَّار** (**kuffār**)

- **تاجر** (**tājir** – merchant) → **تجار** (**tujjār** – merchants)

But, some are irregular, for example :

- **عالم** (**ʿālim** – scholar) → **علماء** (**ʿulamāʾ** – scholars)  
  Persian usage: علماء دين (*ʿulamāʾ-ye dīn* – religious scholars)

Note that there are so many more patterns of broken plurals possible (about thirty in total are documented by grammarians) but these are by far, the most common patterns taken by the most frequent nouns.

**Practical Tips :**

- **Formal vs. casual**: Use broken plurals in writing, titles, and
  formal speech. Use Persian **-hā** in everyday conversation.

- **Iḍāfa**: Broken plurals keep their form in iḍāfa.

- **Gender** : Feminine nouns ending in ة (at) usually form plurals with
  -āt (e.g., **ḥukūmat** → **ḥukūmāt**).

- **Common mistake to avoid**: Do not add **-hā** to a broken plural in
  formal Persian (*kutub-hā* is incorrect; use **kutub**).

Mastering these broken plural patterns will dramatically improve your ability to read and write formal Persian. The Arabic layer of Persian becomes far less mysterious and far more logical once you see the system behind the plurals. If you want to know the deeper logic behind the formation of Arabic broken pluras, click [here](https://www.learnarabiconline.com/broken-plurals/).

------------------------------------------------------------------------

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

6
